evidence_quotes.quote: the use of microtransactions in video games
the shop contains just "random and silly cosmetics" that has nothing to do with the war to entice children to buy "cool cosmetics"

paragraphs.text: Hello, Firstly I would like to say that i am in favour of the "Stop Killing Games" movement, which aims to inform publishers (particularly AAA Video Game publishers) to create video games with a sort of "Backdoor" option in case the game is no longer supported, think of it this way, imagine buying a video game for your son or daughter that is set to go "offline" the week after you bought it and there is no way for them to play it and essentially you just wasted upwards of 80 on a video game that doesn't have any other way of playing it Secondly, I want to turn your attention to the use of microtransactions in video games more specifically the use of microtransctions in games such as "Call of Duty" by Activision, Treyarch and Sledgehammer and "free to play" games such as "War Thunder" by Gaijin Entertainment Call of Duty Microtransactions - Call of Duty (everyone knows it at this point) is a "realistic" view of war from WW2 to recent conflicts such as the gulf war, Now the problem doesn't lie in the depictions of these wars but to the shop system that is implemented, the shop contains just "random and silly cosmetics" that has nothing to do with the war to entice children to buy "cool cosmetics" (now nobody has a problem with "cool cosmetics") but the "cool cosmetics" in question depict characters from animated series such as "American Dad" to more adult programmes such as "The Boys" and "Scream" in a war that had tons of casualties throughout Saddam Hussein's reign. Almost to the point it seems disrespectful to the victims of both sides of the conflict (the older Call of Duty games did not have this problem) War Thunder Microtransactions - War Thunder Microtransactions are the worst in my opinion, these Microtransactions are a way to essentially buy "Premium Vehicles" that will help with the games already slow "grind" (not by much, mind you) the grind is still incredibly long requiring months even years to get to the end of one nations tanks and jets and ships which is where the second issue comes in, you are able to pay to essentially "skip" months of playing to instantly acquire a vehicle and thats a problem, the grind is incredibly long to entice people into paying to either skip the vehicle entirely or buy premium vehicles (which costs up to 80 for one good vehicle by the way) which is essentially pay to win. Now, Gaijin already had one player revolt because of the issues mentioned but they seem to be slipping back into their greedy ways again almost reverting all the changes that made players happy, I also need to mention there is no way to acquire this currency through gameplay, you have to buy the currency out of real money That's all the issues that I have with anti consumer sentiments that gaming developers have, Go raibh maith agat
